title: Arithmetic holonomy bounds and Apery limits
speaker: Vesselin Dimitrov
abstract: A Diophantine upper bound on the dimensions of certain spaces of holonomic functions was the main ingredient in our proof with Calegari and Tang of the 'unbounded denominators conjecture' (presented by Tang in last year's number theory seminar) from the theory of non-congruence and vector-valued modular forms.

proof_stages.label: LCM-denominator holonomic-series problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the basic arithmetic question: classify rational-coefficient power series with prescribed LCM-type denominator growth whose minimal differential equations have singularities only at the normalized points 0, 1, and infinity, noting that the integral-coefficient case is forced to be rational.

proof_stages.label: Modular and vector-valued sources
proof_stages.summary: Modular forms are shown to produce such holonomic functions after rewriting them in a Hauptmodul coordinate, and the bounded-denominator problem is reframed for vector-valued and logarithmic vector-valued modular forms in terms of integral q-expansions, finite monodromy image, and congruence or quasimodular conclusions.

proof_stages.label: Overconvergence and conformal-size growth input
proof_stages.summary: The analytic part of the method uses pullback to universal covers and modular functions, together with conformal-radius and Nevanlinna-type growth estimates, to make singularity restrictions into quantitative overconvergence bounds.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic holonomy dimension bound
proof_stages.summary: After packaging denominator data and local analytic maps into a template, the main holonomy bound asserts that when total conformal size dominates the denominator and approximation costs, the admissible solution space is finite-dimensional over Q(x), consists of holonomic functions, and satisfies an explicit dimension estimate.

proof_stages.label: Low-denominator polylogarithmic classifications
proof_stages.summary: Applying the holonomy-bound philosophy to denominator types arising from integration gives a proved logarithmic classification in the first nontrivial case, a log-squared classification in the next case, and a work-in-progress expectation that the dilogarithm is the only new solution in the Apéry-type denominator-two range.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of period questions to forbidden G-functions
proof_stages.summary: Apéry-limit, logarithmic-product, and dilogarithmic period questions are translated into nonexistence or dimension statements for rational-coefficient holonomic functions with the specified denominator growth and singularity behavior.

proof_stages.label: p-adic zeta-five irrationality contradiction
proof_stages.summary: For the 2-adic zeta value at 5, Eisenstein-type modular forms and overconvergent radius estimates produce a hypothetical G-function if the period were rational, but the holonomy bound allows at most five solutions whereas its derivatives would give six independent ones.

examples.name: A classical modular form written in a Hauptmodul coordinate
examples.note: A concrete modular identity is used to show that an integral q-expansion, though not holonomic as a q-series, becomes holonomic when rewritten as a function of a modular function such as j.
examples.engagement_reason: The classical modular-form/Hauptmodul identity is displayed and interpreted as an identity of q-expansions, but the speaker does not compute with or derive it.
